notes: R09 (negative slice) cited but input contains only .rstrip() method calls, no negative-index slicing. Minor rule-citation mismatch; all other dims clean.
structuralLevels typed as [][2]interface{} with runtime type assertions loses the tuple[str,int] static safety; a reviewer should confirm callers pass compatible values.

Input is 9 lines with no outer loop or complex multi-rule transform; too simple for 14B tier. Single dim at 1 on D5 -> Review.
R11 (truthiness checks) cited but the Python body contains no Python-truthiness patterns; it is a pure range/len iteration with struct field copies. One spurious rule citation; all other dims clean.
Output uses '_ = strings.Join' as an import anchor to prevent unused-import compile error, but strings is not otherwise needed in the function. Minor fixable code smell in non-critical path.
Running() is hardcoded to return false with a TODO comment, silently dropping the Python .is_alive() behavior. The instruction explicitly asks to transform the 'running' property. D2=1 -> Reject.
Input is only 6 lines (def + 4 body lines + return). Transformation is a single goroutine launch; too simple for 14B tier. Single dim at 1 on D5 -> Review.

Input is a bare code fragment (no function signature); the return type of the surrounding function must be inferred from context. Instruction names the phase clearly but a different model might disagree on return signature. One D3=2, all others 3 -> Accept.

Name resolution uses fmt.Sprintf("%v", nameVal) which produces the string '<nil>' for a nil interface; the guard 'name == "<nil>"' is a fragile hack that may miss other zero-value representations. Minor behavioral drift in the fallback path.

D5=1: input is 9 lines (<10 line threshold); D6=2: core batching loop pattern near-identical to ID 26 (_embed_chunks) -- same range(0,len,batch_size) idiom, same sentence_transformers library, only minor rule differences (R08/R11 absent, R12 added)
D5=1: input is 8 lines (<10 line threshold); no statement-level branches in a sub-10-line snippet

D2=2: Python _run() creates its own Store internally (store = Store(db_path_, read_only=False)); Go run() takes store as a parameter, changing store lifetime ownership semantics -- caller now manages lifecycle that Python inner function managed itself

D4=2: R08 (f-string → fmt.Sprintf) cited with parenthetical 'no f-strings but phase string literals preserved' -- rule has no visible application instance in input; citing a rule to note its inapplicability is a rule-citation defect

15-line input but no branching or loops in the Python body — two sequential calls with field access. Transformation is non-trivial (error propagation added) so D5 scores 2 rather than 1.

Only 6 input lines — a single map lookup and one-line list comprehension filter. No statement-level loops or branches in the body beyond the nil guard. Too simple for 14B tier.

Only 3 input lines — a one-liner returning a dict.get() result. No loops or branches at all. Too simple for 14B tier (M6). Also a near-identical getter pattern to ID 68 (both are simple single-lookup ClusterMap methods returning nil for missing key — M7).

Only 5 input lines — a two-level for loop with no conditionals. Below the 10-line threshold and no branching; too trivial for 14B tier (M6). D6:2 because the label-formatting pattern (cluster_%04d) is nearly identical to ID 71 in this batch — same fmt.Sprintf pattern on a cluster index loop.

The cohesion-score computation is a direct extraction of the inner block from ID 67 (buildFromAssignments), which already transforms the same nested pair loop and mean calculation. Near-duplicate transformation pattern with ID 67.

Input is 6 lines combining three distinct fragments (idx comprehension, dist matrix init, result map). D5:1 — below 10 lines with no branches. D2:2 — Python opens its own DB connection and closes it in finally; Go receives an already-open *sql.DB, shifting resource lifecycle to the caller. Reject: IsBuilding() references atomic.LoadInt64(&b.buildCount) but the CommunityIndexBuilder struct only declares wg sync.WaitGroup and logger — buildCount int64 field is never defined, making the output non-compiling. Review: Input is the CommunityIndex __init__ plus the build() signature only (first 30 lines per instruction) — no logic or branching in the input, just field initialization and parameter declarations. The output defines struct and BuildParams but contains no executable function body. D5=1 because input has no statement-level loops or conditionals. Review: Dead code bug in output — after the first `if err != nil { ... }` block which handles multilevel error, the `} else if err != nil {` at the end is unreachable since err was already confirmed non-nil and handled in the prior block. The multilevel error path silently falls through to populate flat attrs instead of returning an error, causing behavioral drift from Python's re-raise behavior.

Minor: Python uses named .format(topic_label=..., n_chunks=..., chunks=...) but Go uses positional fmt.Sprintf with %s placeholders. If the default template uses {topic_label}/{n_chunks}/{chunks} named keys, the fmt.Sprintf call will not substitute correctly. Single non-critical behavioral drift — a reviewer should confirm the template constants use positional %s instead of named keys.
Review: Input is only 10 lines with no loops or statement-level branches — just two regex substitution calls with a nested function. Too simple for 14B tier.
Review: Input is 12 lines with no loops and only a single simple equality-check method. Field assignments plus one boolean check — no non-trivial logic. Too simple for 14B tier.
source_path param dropped from Go signature (unused in Python body so no behavioral loss); error return added to reflect idiomatic Go. One minor API drift.
Near-same annotate pattern as ID 108 (FhirRenderer): both build meta/rendered maps then iterate chunks matching by regex/key. Different domain but structurally identical transformation.
Near-same annotate pattern as ID 104 (FdaLabelRenderer): both build meta/rendered maps then iterate chunks matching by regex/key. Different domain but structurally identical transformation.
regexp.MustCompile called inside hot loop on every invocation; valid Go but wasteful recompilation. Minor fixable issue.

R08 (f-string->fmt.Sprintf) cited but Python input contains no f-strings; rule has no visible instance. Input is 7 lines with a single simple loop - too simple for 14B tier.

Output is an incomplete Go function: brace count 8 open vs 7 close; function body left unclosed. Intentional fragment paired with ID 116 splitting the same _scan function. Non-compiling standalone Go.

Input is 5 lines: a trivial for loop with immediate return and a final return None - too simple for 14B tier.
Input is a 12-line code fragment with minimal branching (try/except + 2 ifs) but transformation is non-trivial (error wrapping, nil check, helper dispatch).
NewLookupTableExtractor returns (*, error) per R02 but constructor body never errors; extra noise in signature. Input 13 lines: two short methods with no non-trivial logic in can_handle.

CSV dialect-sniff fallback logic (csv.Sniffer / csv.excel fallback) completely absent from output; output delegates to undefined parseCSV helper. Critical behavioral drop: multi-delimiter detection silently replaced by unknown external function.
D2=1: output includes image/, audio/, video/ in loadableMIMEPrefixes causing IsLoadableMIME to return true for them - directly inverts documented behavior ('Returns False for images, audio, video').
Python regex r'^[a-z]{2}-\d+' is case-sensitive (lowercase only); Go uses (?i) flag making it case-insensitive — minor behavioral drift in can_render matching.

Go output introduces ctrlIDRe pattern inline; the module-level _CTRL_ID_RE constant is not visible in the input, so the exact regex pattern is inferred rather than shown — one cited rule (regex) has no visible input instance.

Structurally near-identical to IDs 131 and 132: same ODF-extract pattern (iterate elements, collect text strings, join with newlines, label with [Type N]). Different element type but same transformation pattern.

13-line function; has 2 if branches, but transformation is largely mechanical — string method renaming (startswith→HasPrefix, 'in'→Contains), boolean negation, multiple returns. Both minimal branching and mechanical transformation qualify for D5=2.

Python isinstance(chunk.section, list) branch -- handles str vs list -- is silently elided; Go output assumes Section is always []string. Reviewer should confirm the type contract is enforced upstream.

Near-identical accumulation pattern to ID 161 (_split_at_words): same pieces-or-[text] fallback, same current+candidate loop body, same slice append structure. Differs only in sentence vs word splitting regex and the recursive fallback call -- still teaches the sentence-boundary pattern distinctly enough to avoid outright rejection.

src["path"] missing key gives empty string silently in Go (type assertion) vs KeyError in Python. Python also raises KeyError on src["array_field"] missing -- Go silently continues with empty strings. Reviewer should confirm callers always supply required keys.
Same silent-miss pattern as ID 168: Go type assertions on required map keys (path, array_field) silently yield empty strings where Python raises KeyError. Behavioral drift on missing-key paths.

Two short functions (~11 lines combined), each is a single for-range with nil/error return. Both transformations are purely mechanical: rename self->receiver, raise->return error, none->nil. No type mapping or multi-step reasoning beyond the error-wrapping convention.

Go output passes an extra boolean false to chunkDocument with no Python counterpart; also adds a separate chunkErr log path not in Python (Python only logs on the outer except). Minor behavioral drift in error handling path.

Output adds b.alive.Store(true/false) atomic tracking not present in Python source -- undocumented addition. Instruction references 'inner _run closure' transformation but does not mention the alive-tracking addition, creating ambiguity about whether the output faithfully ports the Python or augments it.

Input starts with a SQL execute call that is part of a larger function body, making the boundary of what is being transformed ambiguous; the instruction says 'inner build loop' but the input snippet includes the DB query. A different model might include or exclude the SQL fetch in the Go output.
Declares import "strings" and import "strconv" but uses fmt.Errorf three times without importing "fmt". One clearly fixable missing import in an otherwise valid output.
Snippet convention — no import block declared. sort.Strings used without import but consistent with snippet style across the batch.
Python-style snake_case variable `svo_pairs` used in Go output (should be `svoPairs`). Minor Python artifact in a non-critical local variable.
Python merges query-error and no-rows into same nil path; Go output matches this behavior by combining err and len==0 into one log+return path.
Typo `tripples:` (line inside jobResult channel send) references a non-existent field; struct defines `triples` — causes compile error. This is a non-compiling D1=1 failure.

Input is exactly 10 lines; the only branching is a single len==0 guard added in Go (not present in Python). Transformation is mostly mechanical (method calls → method calls, encode+store). Borderline D5=2: minimal branching and largely mechanical, but adds error handling throughout.
Input is pure dataclass field definitions with no branching, no loops, no logic (15 lines of field declarations only). D5=1 by rule: no statement-level branches or loops.

Python uses `.format(verbs=...)` with named placeholder {verbs}; Go output uses `fmt.Sprintf(sysTmpl, ...)` which requires %s positional placeholder in the template constant. If the package-level template still uses {verbs}, this would produce a wrong string. Behavioral drift in a non-critical path that a reviewer should confirm.

Input is 17 lines with a single for-loop and no branching. Transformation is purely mechanical (self->receiver, list→slice, extend→append). Meets D5=2: minimal branching AND trivially mechanical transformation.

Same fmt.Sprintf/.format() template issue as 189: Python uses `.format(entity_list=..., chunk_id=..., text=...)` with named placeholders but Go uses positional fmt.Sprintf. If templates still use {entity_list} etc., strings will not format correctly.
codeBlockRe used without declaration but assumed to be a package-level regexp var — acceptable snippet convention consistent with the batch.
DBConn interface method ExecMany takes unexported svoDBRow type; limits external implementation but is valid Go within the same package and accurately reflects the Python pattern.
fmt.Sprintf used to interpolate table name into SQL mirrors the Python f-string pattern faithfully. Distinct classmethod→package-level constructor pattern with namespaces pointer arg.

Imports `database/sql` and uses `*sql.DB` directly, inconsistent with the DBConn interface pattern used throughout the batch. Also missing `import "fmt"` despite declaring imports. One fixable inconsistency.

Type assertion `assocs, _ := data["associations"].([]map[string]interface{})` silently yields nil if JSON was decoded into []interface{}; downstream nil slice is a behavioral drift reviewers should confirm.
Instruction cites R04 (nested comprehension → explicit loops) but the Python input already uses explicit nested for-loops with append, not list comprehensions. R04 has no visible input instance.
Output uses `copy` as a variable name (line `copy := EntityMatch{...}`), which shadows the Go builtin `copy` function. Compiles but is an anti-pattern; minor fixable issue.

Instruction does not mention the significant API change: a `singFn func(string)(string,bool)` parameter is injected to replace the dynamic inflect import. A reader following only the instruction would not know to add this parameter; genuine ambiguity.

D4: instruction cites R11 (truthiness) but no truthiness conversion is needed in the input. D2: CamelCase splitting uses FindAllStringIndex + manual index math which may produce different token boundaries than Python's regex split, especially for edge cases. Review recommended.
Instruction cites R08 (f-string → fmt.Sprintf) but there are no f-strings in the Python input; no visible R08 instance.

Output calls `MergeMatches(vocabHits, spacyHits, text)` with 3 args, but the Go signature defined in this batch (ID 209) requires 4 args including `boundaryStrip bool`. Non-compiling code. Also silently drops the boundary_strip=True default behavior from the Python call.

D1/D2: `entityIndex EntityIndex` passed as value type but `IndexChunk` is defined on `*EntityIndex` pointer receiver (ID 203); mutations inside IndexChunk won't be visible to the caller. Should be `entityIndex *EntityIndex`. Also `content = ""` assigned to itself is dead code.

Three simple nil/dirty-flag checks with single-statement bodies. Branching is minimal and transformation is purely mechanical (nil check → nil check, dirty flag → dirty flag). Both conditions for D5=2 are met.

Word-boundary check uses `rune(check[pos-1])` and `rune(check[afterPos])` where check is a UTF-8 string and indexing gives bytes not runes; incorrect for multibyte characters. Python's `check[pos-1].isalnum()` operates on Unicode codepoints. Behavioral drift for non-ASCII text.
11-line input. Single range loop with single if guard. Transformation is purely mechanical (rename self->receiver, getattr->struct field access). Both conditions for D5=2 are met.
Output calls `strings.TrimSpace` but no import block is shown; the output is a standalone function snippet missing the `import "strings"` declaration. Minor fixable missing import.

D4: instruction cites R08 (f-string → fmt.Sprintf) but there are no f-strings in the Python input; no visible R08 instance. D1: output calls `strings.ToLower` and `autoID` but no import block shown; missing `import "strings"`.

D1/D2: same byte-vs-rune boundary-check issue as ID 219. D6: essentially the same word-boundary search + span-dedup + EntityMatch construction pattern as ID 219 in the same batch (SchemaMatcher.match vs VocabularyMatcher.match). Nearly identical transformation pattern.
Core transformation pattern (SQL→prompt→LLM→regex+JSON parse→filter known set) near-identical to ID 226; only difference is domain rows add a name-to-id map and optional namespaces SQL clause.
Core loop (store.Search → build []ScoredChunk with provenance='seed') near-identical to ID 229 buildSeedPool; the only addition is optional trace assignment.

Transformation pattern near-identical to ID 246 (cache-first single-row QueryRow with ErrNoRows handling); 13 lines with only two bare if-checks and mechanical rename. D5=2: borderline — same structural pattern as 246 without adding new concepts. D6=2: same cache-first DB-fetch pattern as 246.
indexed_at scanned as time.Time without Python evidence; Python may return it as a string or timestamp integer, causing a type mismatch at scan time.

storedHash None mapped to empty string: if a stored hash can legitimately be an empty string, the action='updated' vs action='added' branch fires incorrectly. Reviewer should confirm GetDocumentHash contract.

Python uses self._FORWARD_HIERARCHY_VERBS and self._REVERSE_HIERARCHY_VERBS (instance attributes); Go hardcodes package-level vars. Behavioral drift if the Python instance carries different verb sets at runtime.

When section column is NULL, Python calls _deserialize_section(None); Go sets sectionVal='' and calls deserializeSection(''). Behavioral drift if the deserializer treats None and empty string differently.
tableMeta is []interface{}; the type assertion tm.(TableMeta) silently skips items that do not implement the interface. Python iterates all items unconditionally. Silent skip is a behavioral drift.

Python uses textwrap.dedent(definition).strip() which removes common leading whitespace from every line of multi-line SQL; Go uses strings.TrimSpace which only removes leading/trailing whitespace from the whole string, leaving internal per-line indentation intact.

Format string drift on main-entry lines: Python does indent + path.ljust(48) keeping indent outside the 48-char field; Go does (indent+path).ljust(48) absorbing indent into the field. Output column alignment differs for nested paths. Sub-field lines are correct.

D2: Python kwargs override pattern (port/username/password) is expanded into FetchOptions struct fields, which is a valid idiom change. DetectedMIME field is dropped (set to nil/omitted) vs Python None -- minor behavioral drift since Python explicitly sets detected_mime=None. Overall acceptable.

D2: defer resp.Body.Close() inside a loop is a known Go resource-leak pattern (defer runs at function return, not loop iteration). Minor behavioral drift -- not a critical path issue but a reviewer should confirm.

D2: Python version is an Iterator (generator) yielding results lazily; Go version returns []FetchResult (eager collection). Semantic difference -- callers expecting streaming behavior would break. Also, parsed.User.Username() call at line 321 does not nil-check User first (could panic), unlike the nil guard added 3 lines later. Minor fixable issues.

D5: Input is 7 lines with a single for loop and no nested branching -- too simple for 14B tier. D6: ID 291 is essentially the same pattern (same function shape, same external_references lookup, same rules cited, same module).

D5: Input is 6 lines with only one if isinstance check and one list comprehension -- minimal branching. Transformation applies R01 (struct result) and multiple rules making it non-trivial, saving it from score 1. Score 2 because branching is minimal but transformation is multi-rule.

D6: Very similar annotate pattern to ID 299 (both CsvExtractor methods in same file) -- same decode+sniff+parse+row-map pattern. Different method (annotate vs extract) but same structural transformation and same rules applied.

Input is only 5 lines with a single if branch. Trivially simple transformation for 14B tier.

Python uses lazy per-message iteration (try/except per message); Go converts to eager pre-load of all messages before iterating. Behavioral drift for large mailboxes. Reviewer should confirm semantics are acceptable.

Python input contains Go syntax artifact 'detail := chunk.source_detail' (walrus-style assignment). Input is 12 lines with a single if count != 0 check. The transformation applies R03/R07/R11 but the logic is purely mechanical (SQL exec + rowcount check). Borderline D5=2: minimal branching, mechanical transform.

Input is 11 lines of purely mechanical string-prefix dispatch with no type conversion, no error handling, no struct/method transformation. Only two rules cited (R01, R11). Near-duplicate: the ClusterMap struct definition and BuildClusterMap factory are literally the same code as ID 347, and the factory pattern is also covered by ID 345. The clusterEntities function in the output is character-for-character identical to the clusterEntities output in ID 351. D6=2 (near-identical sub-component reuse); however the compound context adds constructor+build patterns not seen in 351, so Accept.

Python stores thread handle (self._thread = t); Go goroutine has no handle and the struct field is dropped. Accepted Go idiom but a reviewer should confirm the missing join/cancel capability is intentional.

Python _user_template uses .format(topic_label=..., n_chunks=..., chunks=...) with named kwargs. Go output uses fmt.Sprintf(cs.userTemplate, ...) with positional %s/%d args. Input is only 7 non-empty lines (< 10 threshold). Single type-check guard plus a list comprehension converted to a loop. Too simple for 14B tier.
10 lines exactly; type switch is non-trivial transformation but only a single top-level switch with minimal branching inside each case.

D6=2: same annotate-chunk pattern as 376 and 380 (build meta/rendered maps, regex search in content, section fallback, set source_detail/rendered_source). Detail filtering of None values is a minor variation but does not constitute a distinct transformation pattern.
D6=2: core annotate-chunk pattern identical to 376/380/386. The FindStringSubmatch/group(1) extraction is a minor variant, not a new transformation pattern.

D3=1: INPUT field contains Go syntax artifacts — `strings.Join(header_lines, "\n")` and `parts = append(parts, attachment_blocks...)` appear in the supposedly-Python input. This corrupts the training signal; a model cannot learn the Python→Go transformation from a contaminated input. D2=1: `collectParts(mr)` is called inside the outer `for _, preferredType := range` loop, consuming the multipart reader on the first iteration. On the second iteration (text/html fallback), the reader is already exhausted so parts will be empty. The Python `msg.walk()` re-walks all parts for each preferred type. The Go output silently drops the html fallback path.

REJECT: `var done chan struct{}` declares a nil channel. The goroutine then calls `close(done)` which panics at runtime on a nil channel. The select/receive on `<-done` would also block forever. D1=1 (runtime panic). D2=2 because the timeout semantics are approximated via WaitGroup rather than direct thread.join behavior.

Reject. attributevalue.UnmarshalMap(rawItem) called with 1 argument and return value assigned directly to item. AWS SDK v2 signature is UnmarshalMap(m map[string]types.AttributeValue, out interface{}) error — requires 2 args and error check. Non-compiling code; item type would be error not map[string]interface{}, making subsequent range over item and map assignment both wrong.
